items.title: Diverse perceptual biases emerge from Hebbian plasticity in a recurrent neural network model
items.author: Francesca Schönsberg, Davide Giana, Yukti Chopra, Mathew E. Diamond, Sebastian Goldt
items.content: <article>Schönsberg et al. show that a simple recurrent neural network with continuous Hebbian plasticity reproduces classic perceptual biases like contraction and repulsion. Tested across three different tasks, the model matches experimental data without fine-tuning, suggesting that distinct biases can arise from a unified local learning mechanism in the brain.</article>

items.title: From variants to mechanisms: Neurogenomics in the post-GWAS era
items.author: Michael P. Margolis, Miao Tang, Miriam Gagliardi, Cindy Wen, Yeda Wu, Naomi R. Wray, Michael J. Ziller, Michael J. Gandal
items.content: <article>Thousands of common genetic variants have been associated with neuropsychiatric disorders, yet their biological impact largely remains elusive. Margolis et al. highlight emerging functional and experimental genomic approaches that connect variants to mechanisms, crucial for developing precision therapeutic interventions.</article>

items.title: Off task map-making: Give it a rest!
items.author: Mark A. Orloff, Erie D. Boorman
items.content: <article>In this issue of Neuron, Xiao, Wang, Zhang, et al. show that the brain is hard at work—especially during rest—integrating separate experiences into a cognitive map, aiding novel decision-making. Using human intracranial recordings, they show that hippocampal ripples during rest predict improved learning, cognitive map formation, and inference.</article>

items.title: I’ve got a friend in you: How the brain socializes during opioid withdrawal
items.author: Zihui Zhang, Matthew B. Pomrenze, Neir Eshel
items.content: <article>Individuals withdrawing from opioids tend to prefer the company of peers who are also withdrawing. In this issue of Neuron, Huo et al. reveal that this preference is driven by corticotropin-releasing hormone signaling in the amygdala, uncovering a new mechanism for addiction-related changes in social behavior.</article>

items.title: Polyserine likes the tau and not the label
items.author: Jay P. Ross, Aaron D. Gitler
items.content: <article>Aggregation of the protein tau is a feature of several neurodegenerative diseases. In this issue of Neuron, Van Alstyne et al. report a polyserine-motif-based strategy to target aggregation inhibitor proteins to tau aggregates, reducing pathology and rescuing neurodegeneration in animal models.</article>

items.title: Glutamate promotes glioma growth via a non-excitable, receptor tyrosine kinase-mediated mechanism
items.author: Xi Huang, Michael D. Taylor
items.content: <article>In this issue, Anastasaki et al. show that glutamate promotes proliferation of pilocytic astrocytoma cells. The authors demonstrate that glutamate receptors activate mitogenic PDGFRα signaling without altering the electrical properties of tumor cells, providing new understanding of how neurotransmitters enhance tumor growth.</article>

items.title: Effects of α-Synuclein Immunization in a Mouse Model of Parkinson’s Disease
items.author: Eliezer Masliah, Edward Rockenstein, Anthony Adame, Michael Alford, Leslie Crews, Makoto Hashimoto, Peter Seubert, Michael Lee, Jason Goldstein, Tamie Chilcote, Dora Games, Dale Schenk

items.title: From prediction to understanding: Will AI foundation models transform brain science?
items.author: Thomas Serre, Ellie Pavlick
items.content: <article>Deep-learning approaches using massive data have transformed AI and are reshaping science. We ask when AI foundation models will transform neuroscience, outlining critical success conditions and a shift from prediction to explanation—linking computations to mechanisms of neural activity and cognition.</article>
